name: Skitch Henderson
abstract: Lyle Russel "Skitch" Henderson (January 27, 1918 – November 1, 2005) was an American pianist, conductor, and composer. His nickname "Skitch" came from his ability to "re-sketch" a song in a different key.

interviews.title: Skitch Henderson interviewed by Monk Rowe, New Milford, Connecticut, October 31, 1998

quotes.text: No. I didn’t know what else to do. There was no more studio. I didn’t fit anyplace. I’m not much of a rocker, and I guess then what I began with was my, I don’t know, stuck in my craw, something so-called I mean — forgive me — legitimate schooling, which is still very important. There was a man who ran Carnegie Hall called Sy Rosen. And after I had my catastrophe when Lenny Bernstein had the Philharmonic and I had this two week train wreck of all time, I recorded with that orchestra. They’re still buried in a vault someplace — with the Philharmonic. Made lots of sides, never to be released, mercifully I suppose. Sy subsequently became manager of the Pittsburgh Symphony and then the Philadelphia Orchestra. And at both places after work when we’d be at the local watering hole, he would say, “Why don’t you try it again sometime?” And my wife, God bless her, registered the title New York Pops. She was so angry that I had stumbled, that she didn’t want the Philharmonic to take the name, see? She said I’ll get even. So she registered that. And we just sat there and then one morning I got a call that the Boston Pops, Arthur had played what he called his beer date, every April, for something like 26 years he had played the same Wednesday night at Carnegie Hall. Never an ad, always sold out. Typical Boston Pops program with Arthur. Then John Williams comes aboard, but he comes aboard with this colossal public relations marketing team who made him the God © Fillius Jazz Archive -16-
quotes.caption: Recalling the creation of the New York Pops name
quotes.doc_title: Skitch Henderson interviewed by Monk Rowe, New Milford, Connecticut, October 31, 1998

quotes.text: Sy Rosen. This same man, Sy Rosen. He now had gone from the Pittsburgh Symphony to the Philadelphia Orchestra where he was manager, to now running Carnegie Hall. And isn’t that something? In that call he gave me a fairly stiff price what his estimate, what it would cost, and we did, as it were, a preview. We did one single concert. It began to rain at three o’clock in the afternoon, and it rained I think until four o’clock the next morning. It was like the South Pacific. But we didn’t have an empty seat. And we lost a good bit of change but it was the start of it, that’s seventeen years ago now. So strange at this time in my life to have such a golden wand of players, but it is a legitimate orchestra. It doesn’t, I mean when we start playing four to the bar I just want to run out of the house. I try to get a small group maybe interspersed from time to time.
quotes.caption: Recalling the first New York Pops concert at Carnegie Hall
quotes.doc_title: Skitch Henderson interviewed by Monk Rowe, New Milford, Connecticut, October 31, 1998

quotes.text: It’s funny, that’s a very good question because sometimes Ruth and I talk about that, if there’s — you know sometimes you get so involved in what you do for a living that you forget everything else. And I really can honestly say, because I’ve always been a nervous wreck all my life. Tomorrow is Armageddon. Because this is that kind of a business. I mean we used to say at NBC there’s a twelve year old walking behind you, he wants your job. I’m serious. And I think I’ve always lived in that factory of insecurity. And I think for the first time, because I have a wonderful orchestra at Carnegie Hall, and I get to guest conduct some wonderful orchestras, and I think for the first time I have almost, I should never say it, but I almost have a comfort. And I think it’s too late for me to try anything else. I’m so bloody lucky that I’ve stumbled through all these transitions of music, I mean starting with territory bands or starting in a roadhouse, and being able to © Fillius Jazz Archive -20-
quotes.caption: General reflection on career security and Carnegie Hall
quotes.doc_title: Skitch Henderson interviewed by Monk Rowe, New Milford, Connecticut, October 31, 1998

about_quotes.speaker: Bucky Pizzarelli
about_quotes.evidence: they asked me to do it with Skitch Henderson... I’m doing a couple of symphony dates with Skitch Henderson in Florida
about_quotes.doc_title: Bucky Pizzarelli interviewed by Monk Rowe, Aspen, Colorado, October 11, 1997 [interview 1 of 2]

about_quotes.speaker: Bob Rosengarden
about_quotes.evidence: He’s an old friend and has never forgotten it, and I’ve never forgotten it.
about_quotes.doc_title: Bob Rosengarden interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, October 6, 1996

about_quotes.speaker: Tommy Newsom and Ross Tompkins
about_quotes.evidence: The interviewer mentions Skitch Henderson in the context of the Tonight Show band ('He was there with Skitch Henderson...'). The interviewee does not claim to have worked with, met, or known Skitch Henderson personally, only responding to the interviewer's statement.
about_quotes.doc_title: Tommy Newsom and Ross Tompkins interviewed by Monk Rowe, Los Angeles, California, September 3, 1995

about_quotes.speaker: Terry Clark
about_quotes.evidence: Skitch Henderson was the conductor of “The Tonight Show,” music director. I said, “Aaron, I tell you. I happen to know”, because we live close to each other. We came to work every day.
about_quotes.doc_title: Terry Clark — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Jimmy Jones
about_quotes.evidence: [INTERVIEWER] ...with Skitch Henderson, NBC, I see here. [INTERVIEWEE] Yeah. Well, I'll tell you, let me explain that some. I was working for -- with John Levy's act.

about_quotes.speaker: Gene Bertoncini
about_quotes.evidence: And Skitch Henderson was on piano. And Peter Appleyard, and a lady bass player, Nicki Parrott, and Joe Ascione and Bucky Pizzarelli.
about_quotes.doc_title: Gene Bertoncini interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, January 13, 2001
